symptom: Indoor head is blinking an error pattern on the operation LED (common: 2 blinks then pause, or 6 blinks). Unit won't start cooling or heating. Remote commands ignored or only briefly accepted.
diagnosis: Communication fault between indoor and outdoor units. Most often: damaged interconnecting wire (S1/S2/S3), loose terminal block screw, or polarity-reversed connection after a prior install/service.
procedure: Read the blink code from the indoor head LED. Reference the service manual: 2 blinks = indoor/outdoor comm, 6 = outdoor PCB fault, 11 = voltage problem.
Kill power to both the indoor disconnect and the outdoor disconnect.
Remove the indoor head's bottom cover to access the terminal block.
Verify S1, S2, S3 wires match at both ends — S1 to S1, S2 to S2, S3 to S3. Reversed polarity is a common installer mistake.
Check each terminal screw for tightness; loose screws arc and degrade over time.
Inspect the interconnect cable for pinches, rodent damage, or UV degradation. Mini-splits run cable outdoors — sun damage is common on installs >5 years old.
Measure DC voltage between S2 and S3 with power on: should be 17-32 VDC pulsing. Zero volts = outdoor board dead or cable open. Steady volts = indoor board not handshaking.
If cable suspect: replace the full run with 14/4 stranded cable, properly strain-relieved at both ends.
If boards suspect: call manufacturer tech line with serial and blink code before quoting parts — boards are $400+ and warranty may apply.
safety_notes: Mini-split comm voltages are low (17-32V DC) but the line-voltage feeding each unit is 208-240V AC. Never work on the terminal block with either disconnect on.
Outdoor condensing units have capacitors that hold charge for minutes after power-off.
Many Mitsubishi models use R32 refrigerant which is mildly flammable — do not work with open flame or sparking tools near an open refrigerant circuit.
